speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape two with Maurice Moore.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your move to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: My life in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your move-- how you moved there. When you were forced to move to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were living in the area that they declared it to be a ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the Jewish neighborhood. And when the war broke out, we were already living in the Jewish neighborhood. And they just put a fence around it. And we were enclosed. And that constitute the ghetto of Warsaw. And we were living right in the center of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't have to move?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have to move.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it just a fence, or was it a wood wall? Or was it a flimsy fence? Or what was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were fences made out of brick. All over, wherever there's an opening, they, they did, they made a-- certain streets were cordoned off. They, they, they built a brick walls. And you couldn't get through. And one side was the ghetto, and the other side is the Polish, the non-- the Aryan side of the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you ever leave the ghetto? Could you ever leave the ghetto? Could Jews ever leave the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We couldn't-- It wasn't-- it was impossible to leave the ghetto. You risked your life to leave the ghetto. Eventually, I left the ghetto. But it was under these circumstances. I risked my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. The ghetto was quite large? Was it quite large?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto? There was a half a million Jewish people living in the ghetto. It was a large part of Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there guards at the gates?

speaker: interviewee
text: The gates, where they used to bring in or take out merchandise or people were guards. There were German guards, Polish guards, and Jewish police.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the hours of entry and exit? And was there a pass system?

speaker: interviewee
text: You could go in and out if you have the proper papers. If the government needed you for some reason, or you worked for the Germans, or for whatever reason. There were a lot of people going in and out all day long. I didn't do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did the rules change in the ghetto very often? The rules of going in and out, or all sorts of rules?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there was always restrictions. And the conditions changed almost daily. One day you could go out because you worked there. The second day you couldn't get out even if you worked there. Every day the conditions changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was living with you? Who was living with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother and my grandma.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have--

speaker: interviewee
text: --in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have your own room or your own apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had an apartment, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The three of you were able to live by ourselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in our apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your apartment like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a two-bedroom apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you weren't squeezed in with a lot of people?

speaker: interviewee
text: We shared our apartment with all the people that came from the small community surrounding the city of Warsaw that were driven by the Germans into the city of Warsaw. We volunteered every room and every inch of our apartment to people, shared whatever food we had with them, shared our belongings, shared everything. Because-- we weren't the only people that did it. Every Jewish family in Warsaw did the same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the problems involved in having people live with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Big problems. We had grown-up people with no children. We have young people with little babies that we couldn't sleep at night. And we had to endure. This were the conditions. They weren't easy. There were cramped people in every corner of the house. There were people sleeping on the floors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have enough to eat? Did you have enough to eat in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had enough to eat because I used to work and make rubber stamps. And I earned a lot of money. And I bought all kind of foods on the black market. So, I-- I did give the money to my grandma. And I didn't care about the money. I just say, here it is. And she bought everything she could lay her hands on.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, we had enough food. The, the only thing that happened, that this working and producing the rubber stamps came to an end. Because the Germans decided that none of the printing presses or people that are able to make printed items should not produce in the ghetto so they can disseminate news of any kind. So they came in one day, and they closed everything up under the threat of death. If I would continue doing-- making these stamps or whatever, I would've been arrested, and maybe who knows what happened to me. And also at that time, my brother was taken by the Jewish police to a camp. That was in March of 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: So, at that time, things changed for me. A fear came over me. And my grandma decided to tell me-- she-- she actually convinced me to leave the ghetto and go wherever, wherever I could in order to not to be taken to a camp. And that's exactly what I did. I left the ghetto on June 22, 1941 with a friend of mine. And his name was Mottel Borensztejn. I remember him now. And we ran away from the ghetto on the other side through a trolley car that made a turn, had to go into the ghetto to turn around. We jumped on the trolley car. We lay down. And we got out on the other side. We bribe-- we had to bribe the conductor.

speaker: interviewer
text: So everything-- the whole way, from the moment we entered the trolley car was nothing but bribes. On every corner that we had to change the trolley car, there were a group of Polish boys waiting for Jewish boys that are trying to, to, to rescue themselves. They actually blackmailed them. So we had to give them money so we can continue our-- to go wherever we want on the direction that we were aiming to go. And we finally came to a place where, through a lot of problems and-- it's a lot of details to really tell the whole story about.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eventually, we wind up in a little city called Plonsk. And from Plonsk we made friends with some Jewish people. And there was no ghetto yet. And that was already-- that particular area was annexed to the German Reich. So there, in that area, they called it the Dritte Reich. And the money in circulation was German marks, not Polish zlotys. Everything was very cheap. And they advised us that the things to do for us, we should go to a farm. And the farmers are looking for hands. And we'll readily get a job. And that's exactly what we did. We went to a farmer, about 10 miles away from the city, and we got-- we got jobs. We did farm work. Whatever the farmer needed us to do, we did. And we worked there for about, maybe this was 1941, until gathering of the-- until after the harvest. After the harvest, the Germans decreed, in case they find a Jewish boy on the farm, that they will take away the farm from the Polak and arrest him. So the Polak, the farmer, told us about the decree, and he let us go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went back to the, to the little city of Plonsk. And at that time, they decided to make a ghetto. So it became a ghetto in Plonsk. And during that day, that time, it was like, I would say July, August, September-- about October. I-- it was either September or October.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was after the harvest. And I had to eat. And the only thing-- I didn't know what to do. I didn't have anybody to give me anything. There was no jobs in the ghetto. So we started smuggling. By the way, my friend went back to the city of Warsaw. And the last time I heard about him that he died in the city of Warsaw from typhus. And I used to go to the farmer and buy different things for fabrics, and buttons, and needles and bring it back to the ghetto. And one day, I fell on the street in the ghetto, and I woke up in a hospital, in a Jewish hospital in the ghetto, two days later. And they told me that I have typhus. That was around November, 1941. And, and I was in the hospital, the Jewish hospital, for 10 days. And thank God, I survived. But when I got out of the hospital, I used-- I walked with two canes. And I was 18 years old. And I was so thin, so emaciated. And I was without a place to go. So I walked on the street. And I used to eat in the soup kitchens that they had for people, out-of-towners. Finally, a family I got acquainted with took me in and gave me a place to sleep. I was all by myself. And little by little, I recuperated. And as I recuperated, I gained strength. I started doing what I did before. I was smuggling from the ghetto into the farm and from the farm to the ghetto. And after I got all my provisions together on my back, and I was walking in the snow for about maybe six hours to get to the ghetto. And when I reached the ghetto walls, a big German shepherd jumped on me. And there was a German police. And he said, oh, so you're a smuggler. I didn't say anything. They just took everything away from me. And they sent me to a camp for smugglers. And not because I'm Jewish, just because, because I was smuggling. It was illegal.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, the camp consisted of 120 Polish men. And among the 120 Polish men, there were two Jews, me and somebody else. And nobody knew that we were Jewish because it's impossible. They didn't know that. They weren't sophisticated people, you know. The only time they knew that you were Jewish was because you wore that Jewish clothes on, you know. You had the black stuff, so they know you were Jewish. But otherwise, they didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyways, it was-- we were working in building highways. We were taking big stones, crushing them with hammers, and taking them to the, to these roads. And this is the way we did. Every day we had a certain amount of stones to crush with hammers. And I finally did it right. And the whole camp got sick on typhus. So when they got sick on typhus, I was already after my typhus that I had in the ghetto. So it did not affect me. But it affected about, I would say, 60% to 65% of the people in the camp. And naturally, if 65% of the people were sick, they didn't eat. So there was plenty of food. So therefore, I recuperated in that particular camp, where I had plenty of food. And I got to be strong.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why wasn't there-- it sounds like there wasn't that much anti-Semitic feeling, or just you said they weren't sophisticated in Plonsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that particular camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the camp? In the ghetto? It didn't seem that hard in the ghetto there.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this wasn't in a ghetto. This was in a camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: For a camp that when the police caught me smuggling, they took me to a camp. And I was-- another fellow who was Jewish and myself, we were among 120 non-Jewish Polish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they didn't seem that interested in finding out who was Jewish and who wasn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: They couldn't tell, because how could they tell?

speaker: interviewer
text: But what about--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we spoke Polish better than they do.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they didn't ask you to drop your pants? You mentioned that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Why should they? They're not the police. Oh, you mean-- oh, you mean the police? No. I was lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you smuggling out of the ghetto to exchange with the farmers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fabrics, needles, and threads. Something that the farmer would buy to make their own clothes. Because the farm women, they used to buy fabric. And they used to make clothes themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they weren't interested in whether you were Jewish or not, the farm people around Plonsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't care. It was strictly business.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What was that ghetto like in Plonsk? Was that smaller?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto was encircled with also a, a wood-- it wasn't exactly like in Warsaw. The fences were a little bit less strong. They were not as strong, strongly built like they would be in a big city. So they were made out of wood. It wasn't a big thing. Some of the boards were loose. You could go in and out. And if they caught you, it was-- it wasn't that bad in Plonsk. So that's the reason I went in and out. There was certain spots, nobody was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after the camp? How did you get out of that camp? And what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that camp, after the people-- there were 10 people that died of typhus. And after they, the rest of them recuperated, they liquidated the camp. And I could've gone any place I wanted to because I got a paper not listing me as a Jew. I got a paper as I was liberated. And I paid my dues to society because I was in that camp, and that I was released. And that's it. That's all it stated.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, I-- but I didn't know. I didn't have any place to go. So I went back to the ghetto in Plonsk. When I went back to the ghetto in Plonsk, after being there for a few days, I went back to the family that gave me lodging. And they gave me lodging all over again. So the local Jewish community, the administration, issued a edict or whatever that anybody that is here illegally, who does not belong to the community, that came from other places, like me, I came from Warsaw, we should register so we can get food stamps. Because without stamps, you had to buy everything on the black market.

speaker: interviewee
text: But if you have legal residence and you get food stamps, the merchandise or the products that you buy, like bread or whatever else you need, provisions, costed very little. So-- because bread on the black market maybe costs 20 times as much. A kilo bread with a coupon costs only 40 pfennigs, like $0.40.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I registered, and so did many other Jewish boys that came from other parts. And in the middle of the night, the Jewish police in the ghetto of Plonsk came to the door, asked for my name, and took me out. And we were destined to go to a camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Instead of getting food stamps, they tricked us. And that's the way they got my name and where I was. So, they took me and 800 other boys. Now they had to deliver to the Germans 800 boys to build a camp. So they didn't have that many people because the city was also, only about 10,000 people was in the city. So to bring out 800 young men, it was a task by itself.

speaker: interviewer
text: So first, they took the people that are not residents, the non-residents. Then they took the local people that are poor. And then they went to the rich people in the little town. And the parents were trying to buy themselves out. So they decided they'll pay like $500 each for their sons not to go to camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the administration-- they used to call it the Judenrat. That was the name of the Jewish administration in the ghetto, Judenrat. The Judenrat could not get enough people. So eventually they got the people's money, and the young men had to go anyways.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to a camp. The camp's name-- the name of the camp was Nosarzewa, which was a little bit-- people that know the area would know it's between Plonsk and Mlawa. And in that camp, we-- I was among the people that were there eight months in that camp. And we built barracks for the German soldiers and a small airport during the eight months.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were seven of the boys that were the children of the rich people of town. They really did not want to work, and they ran away. And about three miles beyond the fence of the camp, they caught them. They brought them back to camp. And they had an appeal, but everybody had to stay and watch. And they hung them. This was in March of 1942. So I witnessed the first hanging of seven young boys, my age 18, 19, in Nosarzewa, in that camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now this camp was set up by the Nazis, the Germans, with the help the Polish Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: How was this camp administered?

speaker: interviewee
text: This camp, all Germans. Everything was German. It was strictly run by Germans. Getting back into the-- I want to interject something about the Warsaw ghetto. Every day in the ghetto, there were many people that dressed up in their best suits and shoes and their clothing because they had no work. And they were very intelligent people. And also at the same time they were poor. They had no money to buy food. And they walked the streets. And every day you could see people falling on the street, dead. They looked prosperous, but they weren't. They were swollen. And trucks used to come by and pick them up, every single day. And that was also prompted me to leave, to leave the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how those people were buried? Were they buried in mass graves?

speaker: interviewee
text: In mass graves, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: By whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the Jewish community. They were the government. Also, the Judenrat, the Warsaw Judenrat-- everybody was buried in a-- there were no individual graves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just out-- just what else did you know about the Judenrats in either Warsaw or Plonsk? Like who was in it and--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Judenrat were these-- when the Germans needed something done, they went to the Judenrat. And they fulfilled the wishes of the Germans. If the Germans needed money, they went to the Judenrat. When they needed people, they went to the Judenrat. So all the, all the people in the Judenrat, the Judenrat which was the administration, and the police, and the FBI, all were Jews. They did the dirty work for the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were the Jewish police under the Judenrat, or were they part of it? The Jewish police?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they were part and parcel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have uniforms, the Jewish police?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had uniforms.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything about those uniforms?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had a regular uniform like a policeman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a color?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like an American policeman.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Did they have insignia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Insignia with the mark of David.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anyone you knew from before?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know in Warsaw I knew many of these people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, in the beginning they just kept order. At the beginning there was no problem. They-- you know, people came. But a lot of people came from outside the city of Warsaw. And they didn't know their way. They didn't know where to go. And they controlled the traffic. It was a regular police force. But then after a while, the Germans utilized their knowledge and their cooperation in order to deliver the Jewish kids to the concentration camps, daily.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you think motivated the Jewish police?

speaker: interviewee
text: What motivated them? They wanted to save their own skin. So they tried-- so they saved their uncles, and their aunts, and their cousins. And their-- they were under-- they were-- they had privileges. And they got food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now back to the camp where you were working in Poland, where you built the barracks. And you said you were going to build an airport there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You built an airport?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was working. They asked me if I can be a carpenter. I said, yes, I can be a carpenter. So I worked as a carpenter. First, I wanted as a helper. And then I was-- got certain responsibilities because there were prefabricated parts for barracks, came from Germany. And all you had to do was just put them together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you-- were you in a camp before that for three months, Nowy Dwor?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in a work camp. Where I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Even earlier

speaker: interviewee
text: --as a smuggler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, that was the name of the smugglers' camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know the name of it. Oh, Nowy Dwor. It was-- I don't know. That's not the name of the camp. But it was near the city of Nowy Dwor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was the smugglers' camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: That, that's the smuggling camp, yeah. Because the other young man, the Jewish boy, he was from Nowy Dwor. That was also the birthplace of my father. So that's the reason I remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father had been born there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we also had people that came to Warsaw and stayed with us in our apartment from that city.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were released from the camp where you were building the barracks and the airport. And you were released back to Plonsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Back to Plonsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: So three times you were in Plonsk.

speaker: interviewee
text: This time I came to Plonsk was in also 1942, around October, the end of October.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, what happened next? How did you get to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Next, we had new things happened in the ghetto. And The Judenrat announced that the ghetto is being liquidated. And all the inhabitants will have to leave the ghetto. They will be transported someplace, to nobody knew where.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, now we're going to stop.
